current_fact.fact_text: On May 5, 1862, Mexican forces under General Ignacio Zaragoza defeated a better-equipped French army at the Battle of Puebla. Though not the end of the war, this unlikely victory became the root of Cinco de Mayo, a celebration of resistance and pride that grew even stronger among Mexican communities abroad.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

current_fact.match_reason: A Mexican-led victory that later became a diasporic celebration fits with an episode exploring Latin-rooted music that crossed borders and reshaped global culture.

queue.fact_text: Europe’s first continental railway opened on May 5, 1835, linking Brussels to Mechelen in Belgium with a track just over 20 km long. Curious locals flocked to watch the noisy steam locomotives, unsure whether humans could safely travel at such ‘dizzying’ speeds of around 40 km/h.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

queue.match_reason: The birth of continental rail travel is matched with an ‘on the move’ episode celebrating motion, journeys, and new ways of moving through the world.

queue.fact_text: On May 5, 1987, millions of Americans watched the Iran–Contra hearings unfold on live television, turning a complex web of secret arms deals and covert funding into a national spectacle. For many viewers, it was the first time they saw political spin, denial, and partial truths dissected in real time.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

Tracklist:
Boban Marković Orkestar – SAT
Anouar Brahem – The Astounding Eyes of Rita
Kikagaku Moyo – Dripping Sun
Son Lux – Lost It to Trying

queue.match_reason: A scandal that forced a country to question its leaders fits with an episode about unlearning, rethinking, and examining what we once accepted without question.

queue.fact_text: On May 5, 1945, a Japanese Fu-Go balloon bomb that had drifted across the Pacific exploded near Bly, Oregon, killing a minister’s pregnant wife and five children on a church picnic. They became the only known civilians killed by enemy action in the continental United States during World War II, victims of a weapon that rode the jet stream.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: A haunting story of unseen forces drifting across oceans connects with an episode about vast skies, long distances, and the quiet power of nature over open water.

queue.fact_text: Italian troops entered Addis Ababa on May 5, 1936, after a brutal campaign that used chemical weapons and mass reprisals against Ethiopian forces and civilians. The so‑called ‘March of the Iron Will’ was staged as a triumphant parade, masking the violence that had paved the road to the capital.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: Italian colonial conquest in Africa is answered with African desert-blues voices of resistance and survival, shifting the focus from empire to those who endure it.

queue.fact_text: On May 5, 1904, legendary pitcher Cy Young threw the first perfect game of modern Major League Baseball, retiring all 27 batters without a single one reaching base. The opposing Philadelphia Athletics didn’t manage even a foul ball into the stands until the seventh inning.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: A perfect game is a kind of seamless, cinematic performance; the smooth, meticulously crafted flow of trip-hop mirrors the calm precision of retiring 27 hitters in a row.

queue.fact_text: Mass protests erupted across Greece on May 5, 2010, as hundreds of thousands of people took to the streets against harsh austerity measures tied to the country’s debt crisis. The demonstrations mixed anger and grief, with chants echoing through Athens as people protested a future being rewritten by spreadsheets and bailout terms.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: Austerity protests are about who pays the cost of economic ‘lessons’; this episode reflects on learning, frustration, and how we might build fairer, more humane ways of improving systems.

queue.fact_text: Kenya Airways Flight 507 crashed into a swamp shortly after taking off from Douala, Cameroon, on May 5, 2007, killing all 114 people on board. Investigators later found that subtle spatial disorientation and miscommunication in the cockpit contributed to the tragedy, a reminder of how fragile our mastery of the skies can be.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: A loss that ends in water and darkness is paired with a gentle, flowing episode about immersion and presence, offering a quiet space to contemplate fragility rather than sensationalize it.

queue.fact_text: The Bishkek Protocol, signed on May 5, 1994, froze the brutal First Nagorno-Karabakh War with a fragile ceasefire rather than a full peace deal. For nearly three decades, life in the region existed in a tense in‑between state—neither war nor real peace, just a line on a map that could ignite at any time.

episode.title: Morning Drops #15 - Unfreezing
episode.description: A short, honest Morning Drop with no grand theme — just four tracks, a bit of voice, and the decision to not wait for the 'right' moment. Room to breathe today.
songs: Jane Birkin & Serge Gainsbourg - Je t’aime… moi non plus
Bernard Badie - In Love (DJ Quad's at Night Mix)

queue.match_reason: A frozen conflict and a ‘ceasefire without peace’ connects with an episode literally titled “Unfreezing,” hinting at the hope of moving beyond suspended tensions.

queue.fact_text: Also on May 5, 1994, American teenager Michael P. Fay was caned in Singapore for vandalism, sparking a diplomatic storm and intense debate about corporal punishment. The case highlighted how radically different legal cultures can collide when one person’s sentence becomes another country’s outrage.

episode.title: Morning Drops #55 - JOMO
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores one of her own personality traits through the lens of psychology and music: the Joy of Missing Out. 

From the Need for Cognition to Deliberate Ignorance and Inattentional Blindness, the episode traces what it means to be someone whose brain demands depth before it gives energy, and what happens when the world around you expects the opposite.

Listen at your own pace, and let us know if you recognize yourself in this one.

Tracklist:
Burna Boy - Ye
2Pac - I Ain't Mad at Cha
Jet Life - Paper Habits
Travis Scott - Stop Trying to Be God
Andrea Laszlo De Simone - Il Regno Animale

queue.match_reason: A sensational international controversy invites reflection on social pressure, norms, and choosing your own lens—echoing an episode about opting out and redefining what you engage with.

queue.fact_text: In many parts of the world, May 5 is marked as World Environment Day’s historical precursor through local nature observances, but more quietly, it is also when the Arctic sun begins its long summer dominance above the polar circle. For weeks, night dissolves into a luminous twilight, and animals recalibrate to a world where darkness almost disappears.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: A fact about Arctic light and ecosystems fits perfectly with an episode built around Arctic waters, whales, and the unique atmosphere above the polar circle.

queue.fact_text: On Sedna, astronomers celebrate May 5 as the anniversary of the first confirmed detection of a micro-meteorite made entirely of industrial space debris, orbiting Earth like a tiny artificial comet. The particle, no larger than a grain of sand, carries layers of old paint, metal, and rocket residue—an accidental time capsule of human spaceflight.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: A drifting shard of man‑made ‘stardust’ circling Earth matches the open, rule‑free, drifting atmosphere of the inaugural Evening Flows journey.

queue.fact_text: Some oceanographers note May as the month when vast phytoplankton blooms often peak in the North Atlantic, turning satellite images into swirling green brushstrokes across the sea. These microscopic plants quietly produce a huge share of Earth’s oxygen, making the ocean a hidden forest that we rarely think of as breathing.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: A story about the hidden life of the ocean and the need to ‘go below the surface’ pairs naturally with a water‑like, immersive musical selection.

queue.fact_text: Culturally, May 5 has become a day when people across the Americas raise glasses and playlists to Mexican heritage, even as historians point out that Cinco de Mayo is more popular in Los Angeles than in many parts of Mexico itself. The celebration is a reminder that traditions can be reborn and remixed far from where they began.

episode.title: Morning Drops #09 - Birthday Cake
episode.description: Today's breakfast comes with a sprinkle of stardust and a slice of birthday cake as we celebrate a pure and unique soul, Yasmin. On Sedna, we may not do years, but we sure know how to party!

queue.match_reason: A cross-border, music-filled celebration of Mexican culture is matched with a birthday-party episode—both are excuses to gather, dance, and reinvent tradition with joy.

published.fact_text: On May 5, 1961, Alan Shepard’s Mercury-Redstone 3 flight made him the first American to travel into space, spending just over 15 minutes in a suborbital arc above Earth. His tiny Freedom 7 capsule had a single window, through which he described the view as “beautiful” while manually steering the spacecraft.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A literal space milestone pairs with an episode about reimagining emptiness as creative space, echoing the sense of launch, exploration, and forward motion.

published.fact_text: When New York’s Music Hall opened on May 5, 1891, the organizers were so proud of their new acoustic masterpiece that they invited Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky to conduct, even though he barely spoke English and found the whirlwind of attention overwhelming. The hall would later be renamed Carnegie Hall and become one of the world’s most iconic music venues.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

published.match_reason: The grand opening of a legendary concert hall mirrors Sedna FM’s own first episode—both are ‘where it all began’ for new musical worlds.

published.fact_text: On May 5, 1973, Secretariat not only won the Kentucky Derby but did so in a blistering 1:59.4, the first horse ever to break the two-minute barrier in that race. Astonishingly, his speed increased every quarter mile—he was still accelerating as he crossed the finish line.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: Secretariat’s record-breaking run feels like a pure electric jolt of speed and power, matching the high-voltage rock energy of “Electric Shock.”

published.fact_text: The 1930 Bago earthquake in Myanmar struck so suddenly that entire sections of Yangon’s cityscape crumbled within seconds, and some churches and pagodas tilted but did not fall, becoming eerie symbols of survival. It remains one of the deadliest earthquakes in the country’s history, with thousands of lives lost in the region between Yangon and Bago.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: After a story of sudden destruction and instability, a calm, downtempo episode offers a sonic ‘slow planet’—a reflective counterpoint about grounding and recovery.

published.fact_text: On his second voyage, Christopher Columbus first sighted Jamaica on May 5, 1494, landing at what he named Discovery Bay. He claimed the lush island for the Spanish crown, unaware that its future identity would be shaped far more by enslaved Africans and music than by any royal charter.

episode.title: Morning Drops #29 - Morning Dubs
episode.description: Recorded while still processing Houghton Festival. One of the mornings there, waking up in the woods to a reggae DJ set — no phone signal, just trees, music, and loved ones. A slow selection of reggae, dub, and morning rhythms. Dubs instead of drops. Start your day slow — with space to breathe.
songs: The Gaylads - Joy in the Morning
King Tubby - King Tubby Meets the Rockers Uptown

published.match_reason: The colonial ‘discovery’ of Jamaica is paired with an episode steeped in reggae and dub, connecting the island’s deeper cultural legacy to its modern musical voice.

published.fact_text: Napoleon Bonaparte died in exile on remote Saint Helena on May 5, 1821, thousands of kilometers from the European capitals he once dominated. The island’s isolation was so complete that it took weeks for news of his death to reach Europe, turning his final years into a kind of living ghost story.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

published.match_reason: A fallen emperor reflecting in exile parallels an introspective episode about looking back on difficult seasons and finding a different kind of strength.

published.fact_text: On May 5, 1877, the Lakota leader Sitting Bull led his people across the border into Canada, seeking refuge from relentless U.S. Army pursuit. For a brief period, a sovereign Indigenous nation existed in motion, living between frontiers rather than inside any line on a map.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: Sitting Bull’s mobile resistance and search for refuge resonate with Tuareg music born of exile, borders, and survival in harsh lands.

published.fact_text: During the Iranian Embassy siege in London, British SAS commandos launched Operation Nimrod on May 5, 1980, abseiling down the building and blowing out windows live on television. The 17-minute assault was so swift and precise that it reshaped global perceptions of counterterrorism units overnight.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A tense, televised hostage rescue fits with the explosive, politically charged fury of Rage Against the Machine and their focus on state power and resistance.

published.fact_text: By May 5, 1940, the small Norwegian garrisons at Hegra Fortress and Vinjesvingen finally surrendered to invading German forces after weeks of isolated resistance. Their stand became a symbol in Norway of stubborn defiance in the face of overwhelming odds.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A story of grim resistance and eventual surrender is matched with an episode about storms passing and hope persisting—‘it can’t rain forever’ even in wartime darkness.

published.fact_text: The Stratton Brothers trial that began on May 5, 1905, in London was the first time fingerprints were used to secure a murder conviction. A single bloody thumbprint on a cash box lid quietly ushered in the modern era of forensic science, turning human skin patterns into courtroom evidence.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

published.match_reason: Fingerprint forensics and Auto-Tune both mark quiet technological revolutions—tools that moved from invisible background to controversial center stage in their fields.

published.fact_text: On May 5, 1862, Mexican forces under General Ignacio Zaragoza defeated a better-equipped French army at the Battle of Puebla. Though not the end of the war, this unlikely victory became the root of Cinco de Mayo, a celebration of resistance and pride that grew even stronger among Mexican communities abroad.

episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar
